name: Parse CLI Arguments with getopts and Long Options
description: Handle short and long command-line options, required arguments, and positional parameters in bash scripts using getopts and case statements.

steps.title: Parse short options with getopts
steps.description: Use the built-in getopts for simple short option parsing with flags and arguments.
steps.tip: The colon after an option letter (like o:) means that option requires an argument.

steps.title: Add long option support with a case statement
steps.description: Handle --long-option style flags using a while/case loop.

steps.title: Validate required arguments
steps.description: Check that required options and positional arguments are provided.
steps.command: echo '[[ -z "$OUTPUT" ]] && { echo "Error: --output is required" >&2; exit 1; }' && echo '[[ ${#FILES[@]} -eq 0 ]] && { echo "Error: at least one file required" >&2; exit 1; }'

steps.title: Test the argument parser
steps.description: Run the script with various option combinations to verify parsing.

faq.question: Should I use getopts or a while/case loop?
faq.answer: Use getopts for simple scripts with short options only. Use a while/case loop when you need long options (--verbose), equals syntax (--output=file), or complex validation. Most production scripts use the while/case pattern.

faq.question: How do I handle --option=value syntax?
faq.answer: Add a pattern like '--output=*') OUTPUT="${1#*=}"; shift ;;' which strips everything before and including the = sign to extract the value.

faq.question: What does -- (double dash) mean?
faq.answer: Double dash signals the end of options. Everything after -- is treated as positional arguments, even if it starts with a dash. This lets users pass filenames like '-weird-name.txt' without it being interpreted as an option.
